body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: gap:slash-command (equivalently gap:reset_session)

## Scenario

session-reset: a session runs a no-op turn, then the user invokes a /new (or /clear) reset WITHIN the same running session; the old session row disappears from the dashboard and a NEW session_id appears in ready. User-observable signal (scenario-meanings): the old session ends and a fresh session_id is born, all inside one driven run — distinct from session-end + session-start (separate processes/invocations).

## Agent supports it (agent_supports: yes)

opencode's interactive TUI ships a new-session command — the bundled binary carries 131 `session.new` references plus a `New session` command-palette entry (`/Users/ingo/.opencode/bin/opencode`). So in a live TUI the user CAN reset the conversation to a fresh `ses_…` row mid-session, exactly the /new-style reset this scenario exercises. The capability exists in the agent.

## daemon observes it (daemon_capability: full)

opencode is the lone `ProcessOwnedStore` adapter (`core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/agent.go`): the daemon reads session state straight from the SQLite store, and a new `ses_…` row materializing is exactly what the session-resume cell already proves the daemon observes live (a fresh session_id appearing → ready). A TUI /new mints a second `ses_…` row under the same PID; the daemon would surface the new session in ready and retire the old row via the same-PID cleanup, just as the codex/pi supersession shape does. The daemon is NOT the blocker.

## Why this cell is NOT recordable today (driver gap: slash-command / reset_session)

The opencode interactive driver (`.claude/skills/ir:onboard-agent/scripts/drive-opencode-interactive.sh`) drives opencode through BLOCKING headless `opencode run` subprocesses and implements ONLY three step types in its `case "$TYPE"` dispatch: `send` (launches an `opencode run` subprocess and WAITS for it to complete), `wait_turn` (a no-op — `opencode run` already blocks until the turn ends), and `sleep`. There is NO step that processes an in-TUI slash command and NO `reset_session` step to clear the cached session id and re-point subsequent turns at a freshly minted `ses_…`.

Two independent blockers, both proven:

1. **Headless `opencode run` ignores in-TUI slash commands.** A `send`-shaped recipe that sends `/new` (or `/clear`) LINTS in-grammar but is a semantic no-op. PROBED 2026-05-26 in an isolated XDG_DATA_HOME: `opencode run "/new"` created exactly ONE `ses_…` row and stored the literal text `/new` as a normal USER message with an assistant reply — it was NOT interpreted as a reset; no second session was minted. This matches the earlier sweep finding that an inline `/model` was ignored and the turn ran on the default model. Slash commands are a live-TUI primitive, not a headless-`run` primitive.

2. **No reset_session step.** Even if a slash command could be delivered, the driver caches a single `SESSION_ID` and writes one `session.uuid`; it has no step to detect a /new-spawned second `ses_…` and re-arm transcript resolution onto it. The codex/pi rows for this scenario use a `reset_session` step for exactly this; the opencode driver has no such step.

### Not a false gap (and not caught by recipe-lint)

The recipe is left `applicable:false` with no `script`, so there is no step-TYPE for recipe-lint.sh to reject — the gap is the absence of (a) a way to deliver an in-TUI slash command and (b) a `reset_session` capability, mirroring the opencode user-esc-interrupt gap:interrupt and model-switch-midsession gap:model-select, which recipe-lint likewise could not catch. A /new reset is fundamentally a live-TUI primitive against a running session; there is no send-only equivalent, because headless `opencode run` treats `/new` as prompt text (proven above).

## What would unblock it

Replace or augment `drive-opencode-interactive.sh` with a LIVE-REPL driver (analogous to the tmux-based claudecode/pi/aider drivers, or a codex-style multi-session capture): hold the opencode TUI open, send the first prompt, deliver the New-session command, then track the second `ses_…` and re-point subsequent turns at it (a `reset_session` step that re-arms resolve_transcript onto the new session slot, plus a `session.uuids` PLURAL list so curate-lifecycle-fixture.sh keeps both rows). Once the driver can reset within a live session, the cell becomes recordable — the daemon already observes a fresh `ses_…` appearing in ready.

## Matrix drift

The coverage matrix currently records opencode/session-reset as supports=unknown (recording deferred), framed as 'session-end + session-start, not session-reset'. That under-states the agent: opencode's TUI DOES ship a /new reset (`session.new`), so agent_supports is yes; the true blocker is driver_capability: gap:slash-command (no in-TUI slash delivery + no reset_session step), not an agent limitation. The coverage cell should be updated to agent_supports:yes, driver_capability:gap:slash-command.

## Confidence

0.85: the driver grammar (`send|wait_turn|sleep`, blocking `opencode run` per send, no slash/reset_session step) is read directly from the live driver source; the headless-slash no-op is empirically PROVEN by the isolated `opencode run "/new"` probe (one session, `/new` stored as user text); and the agent's New-session capability is read from the binary. Not higher only because the unblocked recording (a live /new minting a second ses_… that the daemon retires the old row for) has not been captured — that is the developer task this gap defers.
caveats: Driver-gap, not an agent or daemon limitation: opencode the agent supports a /new reset (binary carries `session.new` / `New session`) and the daemon reads session state from the SQLite store (a fresh ses_… in ready is the same signal session-resume already validates). The blocker is purely the driver's missing in-TUI slash-command delivery + reset_session primitive (gap:slash-command).

PROBED 2026-05-26: headless `opencode run "/new"` in an isolated XDG_DATA_HOME created exactly ONE ses_… row and stored the literal text `/new` as a normal user message (with an assistant reply) — it was NOT interpreted as a reset; no second session minted. Slash commands are a live-TUI primitive, not a headless-`run` primitive.

recipe-lint.sh does NOT catch this gap: the recipe is left applicable:false with no script, so there is no step-TYPE to reject; the missing capability is in-TUI slash delivery + a reset_session step (same shape as the opencode user-esc-interrupt gap:interrupt and model-switch-midsession gap:model-select).

sources.note: case "$TYPE" handles only send|wait_turn|sleep; each `send` launches a BLOCKING `opencode run` subprocess; no step processes an in-TUI slash command and no reset_session step to clear/re-point the cached session id mid-script
opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter; daemon reads session state from SQLite, so a fresh /new-spawned ses_… WOULD be observed in ready (same signal session-resume validates)
sibling: daemon observes a new ses_… row appearing in ready live (daemon_capability:full); the daemon is not the blocker for session-reset either

claudecode/codex session-reset recipes use a `reset_session` step (detect the second session, re-point subsequent turns); the opencode driver has no reset_session step and no live REPL to deliver /new into — it shells out to `opencode run` per turn
created one ses_… row; the literal `/new` was stored as a user message with an assistant reply — proves headless `opencode run` treats slash commands as prompt text, not as TUI reset commands
131 `session.new` references + `New session` command entry — opencode's TUI ships a /new reset, so agent_supports:yes
coverage cell currently supports=unknown (recording deferred); should update to agent_supports:yes, driver_capability:gap:slash-command
